title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: ggbasemap R package makes easy add basemap tiles XYZ tile servers (like OpenStreetMap, Google, ESRI) ggplot2 plots. automatically detects bounding boxes, calculates appropriate zoom levels, handles technical details fetching displaying tiles.

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: Automatic zoom calculation: need guess right zoom level Multiple tile sources: Works OpenStreetMap, Google, ESRI, CartoDB, Image transformations: Grayscale, saturation, brightness, contrast, gamma Map rotation: Rotate maps angle using Oblique Mercator projection Web Mercator native: Tiles kept EPSG:3857 sharpest display sf compatible: Works seamlessly sf objects

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
code: # Install from GitHub devtools::install_github("yourusername/ggbasemap")  # Or from local source devtools::install("path/to/ggbasemap")

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: simplest way use ggbasemap sf object: : 1. Extract bounding box nc 2. Calculate optimal zoom level 3. Download tiles OpenStreetMap 4. Display background layer
code: # Load required libraries library(ggbasemap) library(ggplot2) library(sf)  # Read some spatial data nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package = "sf"), quiet = TRUE)  # Create a map with basemap ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red", linewidth = 1)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: data data frame longitude latitude columns:
code: df <- data.frame(   city = c("NYC", "Boston", "Philadelphia"),   lon = c(-74.006, -71.0589, -75.1652),   lat = c(40.7128, 42.3601, 39.9526) )  ggplot() +   add_basemap(df, "lon", "lat") +   geom_point(data = df, aes(x = lon, y = lat),               color = "red", size = 3) +   geom_text(data = df, aes(x = lon, y = lat, label = city),             vjust = -1, color = "white")

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: can easily switch different tile providers:
code: # OpenStreetMap (default) ggplot() + add_basemap(nc)  # Google Satellite ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,      url = "https://mt1.google.com/vt/lyrs=y&x={x}&y={y}&z={z}")  # ESRI World Imagery ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,     url = "https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{z}/{y}/{x}")  # CartoDB Positron (clean, light) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,     url = "https://a.basemaps.cartocdn.com/light_all/{z}/{x}/{y}.png")  # OpenTopoMap (topographic) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,     url = "https://a.tile.opentopomap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png")

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: data: sf object data frame containing spatial data x, y: Column names longitude latitude (data frames) bbox: Optional bounding box override automatic detection url: Tile server URL template zoom: Zoom level (auto-calculated provided)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: alpha: Transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = opaque) interpolate: Whether interpolate pixels (default: TRUE) padding: Extra space around data (default: 0.1 = 10%)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: grayscale: Convert black white (TRUE/FALSE) saturation: Color saturation (0 = grayscale, 1 = normal, >1 = vivid) brightness: Brightness multiplier (1 = normal, <1 = darker, >1 = brighter) contrast: Contrast adjustment (1 = normal, <1 = flat, >1 = punchy) gamma: Gamma correction (1 = normal, <1 = brighter midtones, >1 = darker midtones)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
code: # Just add basemap to your ggplot ggplot() +   add_basemap(my_data) +   geom_sf(data = my_data)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
code: # Adjust transparency and sharpness ggplot() +   add_basemap(my_data, alpha = 0.7, zoom = 15) +   geom_sf(data = my_data, fill = NA, color = "red")

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
code: # Create a specific mood ggplot() +   add_basemap(my_data,               grayscale = TRUE,               brightness = 0.9,               contrast = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = my_data, color = "white")

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
code: # Force a specific zoom level ggplot() +   add_basemap(my_data, zoom = 14) +   geom_sf(data = my_data)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: package auto-calculates zoom level, sometimes want control: Low zoom (5-8): Regional/country views Medium zoom (9-12): City/state views High zoom (13-16): Neighborhood/street views high (17-19): Building-level detail

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: large geographic areas, consider:
code: # Use lower zoom for large areas ggplot() +   add_basemap(big_country, zoom = 6, padding = 0.05) +   geom_sf(data = big_country)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
code: ggplot() +   # Base layer: basemap   add_basemap(my_data, alpha = 0.6) +   # Middle layer: boundaries   geom_sf(data = boundaries, fill = NA, color = "black") +   # Top layer: your data   geom_sf(data = my_data, aes(color = category), size = 2) +   # Legend and theme   scale_color_viridis_d() +   theme_minimal()

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
code: p <- ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc) +   geom_sf(data = nc)  # Save with high resolution ggsave("my_map.png", p, width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 300)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: get “fetch tiles” errors: Check internet connection Try different tile URL corporate networks block tile servers Use verbose = TRUE see ’s happening:
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(my_data, verbose = TRUE) +   geom_sf(data = my_data)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: auto-zoom adds +1 sharper images, can increase :
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(my_data, zoom = 16) +  # Force high zoom   geom_sf(data = my_data)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: data getting cut , increase padding:
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(my_data, padding = 0.3) +  # 30% extra space   geom_sf(data = my_data)

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: Learn Map Rotation create angled maps Explore Image Transformations styled basemaps See Advanced Usage power users Check FAQ common questions

title: Introduction to ggbasemap
text: ggplot2 documentation sf package documentation ggspatial documentation Slippy Map Tilenames - Technical details XYZ tiles

title: Map Rotation
text: Map rotation allows present spatial data angle, can useful : Fitting rectangular maps better page layouts Emphasizing certain geographic features Creating visually interesting presentations Aligning natural geographic orientations (e.g., coastlines, mountain ranges) ggbasemap uses Oblique Mercator projection achieve smooth rotation maintaining accurate geographic relationships.

title: Map Rotation
text: rotate map, use coord_rotate() function:
code: # Get rotation parameters for 45 degrees rot <- coord_rotate(nc, angle = 45)  # Create the rotated map ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot$coord +   ggtitle("North Carolina rotated 45°")

title: Map Rotation
text: coord_rotate() function returns list : coord: coord_sf object applies rotation bbox: expanded bounding box covers rotated area crs: coordinate reference system string used angle: rotation angle degrees

title: Map Rotation
text: rotate map, corners extend beyond original bounding box. bbox returned coord_rotate() expanded ensure entire rotated map covered: expanded bbox used add_basemap() fetch enough tiles cover entire rotated view.
code: # Original bbox orig_bbox <- sf::st_bbox(nc) print(orig_bbox)  # Rotated bbox (notice it's larger) rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45) print(rot$bbox)

title: Map Rotation
text: rotation uses Oblique Mercator projection parameters: lat_0: Latitude centroid lonc: Longitude centroid gamma: Rotation angle degrees alpha: Zero (skew) k: Scale factor (1.0) projection preserves: - ✅ Local shapes angles - ✅ Straight lines (great circles) - ✅ Scale along central line

title: Map Rotation
code: # No rotation (0°) rot0 <- coord_rotate(nc, 0) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot0$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot0$coord +   ggtitle("0° (No Rotation)")  # 30° rot30 <- coord_rotate(nc, 30) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot30$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot30$coord +   ggtitle("30° Rotation")  # 45° rot45 <- coord_rotate(nc, 45) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot45$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot45$coord +   ggtitle("45° Rotation")  # 60° rot60 <- coord_rotate(nc, 60) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot60$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot60$coord +   ggtitle("60° Rotation")  # 90° rot90 <- coord_rotate(nc, 90) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot90$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot90$coord +   ggtitle("90° Rotation")

title: Map Rotation
text: can also use negative angles clockwise rotation:
code: # -45° (clockwise) rot_neg45 <- coord_rotate(nc, -45) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot_neg45$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot_neg45$coord +   ggtitle("-45° (Clockwise)")

title: Map Rotation
text: Angles greater 360 automatically normalized:
code: # 405° = 45° rot405 <- coord_rotate(nc, 405)  # Same as 45°

title: Map Rotation
text: Sometimes data oriented angle doesn’t fit well standard rectangular plot:
code: # Create elongated data elongated_data <- nc  # Original (wastes space) ggplot() +   add_basemap(elongated_data) +   geom_sf(data = elongated_data) +   ggtitle("Original (wasted space on sides)")  # Rotated (better fit) rot <- coord_rotate(elongated_data, 30) ggplot() +   add_basemap(elongated_data, bbox = rot$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = elongated_data) +   rot$coord +   ggtitle("Rotated 30° (better fit)")

title: Map Rotation
text: coastal areas, rotating align coastline can visually effective:
code: # Hypothetical coastal data rot <- coord_rotate(coastal_data, -15)  # Align with coastline ggplot() +   add_basemap(coastal_data, bbox = rot$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = coastal_data) +   rot$coord +   ggtitle("Aligned with Coastline")

what: Example 3: Emphasizing a Road or River
title: Map Rotation
text: Rotate align linear features:
code: # Align with a major highway rot <- coord_rotate(region_data, 25) ggplot() +   add_basemap(region_data, bbox = rot$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = major_road, color = "red", size = 2) +   geom_sf(data = region_data, fill = NA) +   rot$coord

title: Map Rotation
text: Rotation works perfectly image transformations:
code: rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)  ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,                bbox = rot$bbox,               grayscale = TRUE,               contrast = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   rot$coord +   ggtitle("Rotated + Styled")

title: Map Rotation
text: can add multiple layers top rotated basemap:
code: rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 30)  ggplot() +   # Base: rotated basemap   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox, alpha = 0.7) +   # Layer 1: county boundaries   geom_sf(data = nc, aes(fill = area), alpha = 0.5) +   # Layer 2: major roads   geom_sf(data = roads, color = "red", size = 1) +   # Layer 3: cities   geom_sf(data = cities, aes(size = population), color = "blue") +   # Apply rotation to all   rot$coord +   scale_fill_viridis_c() +   theme_void()

title: Map Rotation
code: rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)  ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,                bbox = rot$bbox,               zoom = 12,               saturation = 0.5) +   geom_sf(data = nc,            fill = NA,            color = "white",           linewidth = 1) +   rot$coord +   theme(     panel.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),     plot.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),     panel.grid = element_blank()   ) +   ggtitle("Night Mode Style",           subtitle = "45° rotation") +   theme(     plot.title = element_text(color = "white", size = 20),     plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray70", size = 14)   )

title: Map Rotation
text: package also provides rot() function creates 2D rotation matrix: used internally can useful custom calculations.
code: # Create rotation matrix for 45 degrees m <- rot(45) print(m)  # Apply to points points <- matrix(c(1, 0, 0, 1), ncol = 2) rotated_points <- points %*% m

title: Map Rotation
text: Rotated maps require tiles bounding box expanded: 0° rotation: Minimum tiles 45° rotation: ~1.4x tiles (diagonal expansion) rotation: tiles rotation means: - Rotated maps load slightly slower - Use reasonable zoom levels - Cache results possible

title: Map Rotation
text: expanded bounding box means: - Larger raster objects - memory usage - Slower rendering large areas high zoom levels, consider: - Using lower zoom (11-13 instead 15+) - Cropping data rotation - Using padding = 0.05 instead default 0.1

title: Map Rotation
text: Problem: Parts data cut Solution: expanded bbox prevent , :
code: # Increase padding manually rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45) # Expand bbox further rot$bbox <- rot$bbox * 1.1  # Add 10% more  ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot$coord

title: Map Rotation
text: Problem: Rotation works tiles don’t appear Solution: Check ’re using bbox coord_rotate():
code: # Wrong - uses original bbox ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc) +  # Missing bbox!   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot$coord  # Correct - uses expanded bbox ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +  # Correct!   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot$coord

title: Map Rotation
text: Problem: Text labels map rotate map Solution: expected! Everything rotates together. need horizontal text, add applying coordinate system annotate() use geom_text() hjust vjust adjustments.

title: Map Rotation
text: 0-15°: Subtle, good minor adjustments 15-45°: Moderate, good fitting page layouts 45-60°: Strong rotation, dramatic effect 60-90°: strong, use sparingly

title: Map Rotation
code: # Try several angles to find the best angles <- c(0, 15, 30, 45) plots <- lapply(angles, function(a) {   rot <- coord_rotate(nc, a)   ggplot() +     add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +     geom_sf(data = nc) +     rot$coord +     ggtitle(paste(a, "degrees")) })  # View all with patchwork or gridExtra

title: Map Rotation
text: Academic papers: Subtle rotation (0-20°) Presentations: Moderate rotation (20-45°) Marketing/Design: angle works

title: Map Rotation
text: Rotation works great : - Image transformations (grayscale, contrast) - Different tile sources - Multiple data layers - Custom themes

title: Map Rotation
text: ’s complete, publication-ready example:
code: library(ggbasemap) library(ggplot2) library(sf) library(viridis)  # Load data nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package = "sf"))  # Calculate rotation (30 degrees looks good for this shape) rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 30)  # Create the map map <- ggplot() +   # Base layer: styled basemap   add_basemap(nc,                bbox = rot$bbox,               zoom = 9,               saturation = 0.4,               brightness = 1.05,               contrast = 1.1) +   # Data layer: counties with fill   geom_sf(data = nc,            aes(fill = AREA),           color = "white",           linewidth = 0.3,           alpha = 0.8) +   # Apply rotation   rot$coord +   # Color scale   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma",                        name = "Area (sq km)",                        labels = scales::comma) +   # Theme   theme_void() +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"),     plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),     plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, hjust = 0.5, color = "gray40")   ) +   # Labels   labs(title = "North Carolina Counties",        subtitle = "Rotated 30° for optimal layout",        caption = "Data: North Carolina county boundaries")  # Save #ggsave("nc_rotated_map.png", map, width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 300)  print(map)

title: Map Rotation
text: Read Image Transformations style basemaps Check Advanced Usage power user features See FAQ common questions

title: Map Rotation
text: Snyder, J. P. (1987). Map Projections: Working Manual OpenStreetMap Wiki: Mercator Projection PROJ documentation: Oblique Mercator

title: Image Transformations
text: ggbasemap provides powerful image transformation capabilities allow style basemaps match visualization needs. can adjust grayscale, saturation, brightness, contrast, gamma correction.

title: Image Transformations
text: Convert basemap black white classic look: perfect : - Academic publications - Print media - want data stand - Creating vintage-style maps
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, grayscale = TRUE) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 1) +   theme_void()

title: Image Transformations
text: Control intensity colors: Use cases: - saturation = 0: Documents, printing - saturation = 0.3-0.7: Subtle, professional look - saturation = 1: Normal (default) - saturation = 1.2-2: Vibrant, eye-catching maps
code: # No saturation (black & white) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 0) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Saturation = 0")  # Reduced saturation (50%) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 0.5) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Saturation = 0.5")  # Normal saturation ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 1) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Saturation = 1 (default)")  # Increased saturation (150%) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 1.5) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Saturation = 1.5")

title: Image Transformations
text: Adjust overall lightness: Tips: - Use brightness < 1 light-colored data - Use brightness > 1 dark-colored data - Combine contrast best results
code: # Darker (-30%) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, brightness = 0.7) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "yellow") +   ggtitle("Brightness = 0.7 (darker)")  # Normal ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, brightness = 1) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Brightness = 1 (default)")  # Brighter (+30%) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, brightness = 1.3) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "darkblue") +   ggtitle("Brightness = 1.3 (brighter)")

title: Image Transformations
text: Enhance reduce difference light dark areas: use: - contrast < 1: Soft, dreamy aesthetics - contrast > 1: Sharp, dramatic effect - contrast = 1.2-1.4: Good satellite imagery
code: # Low contrast (flat) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, contrast = 0.6) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Contrast = 0.6 (flat)")  # Normal ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, contrast = 1) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Contrast = 1 (default)")  # High contrast (punchy) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, contrast = 1.5) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Contrast = 1.5 (punchy)")

title: Image Transformations
text: Adjust midtones without affecting blacks whites: Technical note: Gamma correction non-linear applied first, brightness contrast.
code: # Gamma < 1: brighter midtones (good for dark imagery) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, gamma = 0.8) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Gamma = 0.8 (brighter midtones)")  # Gamma = 1: no change ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, gamma = 1) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Gamma = 1 (default)")  # Gamma > 1: darker midtones (good for washed-out imagery) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, gamma = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +   ggtitle("Gamma = 1.2 (darker midtones)")

title: Image Transformations
text: real power comes combining multiple transformations:

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               saturation = 0.3,      # Desaturate               brightness = 0.85,     # Slightly darker               contrast = 1.2,        # More contrast               gamma = 1.1) +         # Darker midtones   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "brown", linewidth = 0.8) +   theme_void() +   ggtitle("Vintage Style")

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               grayscale = TRUE,      # Black & white               brightness = 1.1,      # Brighter               contrast = 1.6) +      # Very contrasty   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "black") +   theme_void() +   ggtitle("High-Contrast Monochrome")

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               saturation = 0.6,      # Muted colors               brightness = 1.1,      # Brighter               contrast = 0.8,        # Less contrast               gamma = 0.9) +         # Brighter midtones   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "darkblue") +   theme_void() +   ggtitle("Soft/Faded Look")

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               saturation = 0.7,               brightness = 0.6,      # Much darker               contrast = 1.3,               gamma = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "cyan") +   theme_void() +   ggtitle("Night Mode")

title: Image Transformations
text: want data star:
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               saturation = 0.2,      # Almost grayscale               brightness = 1.05,               contrast = 0.9) +   geom_sf(data = nc,            aes(fill = area),          # Your data stands out           color = "white",           linewidth = 0.3) +   scale_fill_viridis_c() +   theme_void() +   ggtitle("Data-Friendly Background")

title: Image Transformations
text: Transformations applied order: Gamma (non-linear correction) Brightness (linear multiplication) Contrast (centered 0.5) Saturation (color adjustment) Grayscale (final conversion TRUE) order ensures best visual results.

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(data,               grayscale = TRUE,      # Always use B&W for print               contrast = 1.2,        # Boost contrast for printing               brightness = 0.95) +   # Slightly darker for better ink coverage   geom_sf(data = data)

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(data,               saturation = 1.1,      # Slightly more vibrant               brightness = 1.05,     # Brighter for screens               contrast = 1.0) +      # Normal contrast   geom_sf(data = data)

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(data,               url = "...satellite...",               contrast = 1.3,        # Enhance details               gamma = 0.9,           # Brighten shadows               saturation = 1.2) +    # Vivid colors   geom_sf(data = data)

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(data,               saturation = 0.4,      # Muted               brightness = 1.1,      # Light background               contrast = 0.9) +      # Softer   geom_sf(data = data)

title: Image Transformations
text: Don’t overdo : Extreme values (>2 <0.3) usually look bad Check data: Make sure data colors work basemap Test different screens: looks good monitor might others Save care: Transformations affect file size quality

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, grayscale = TRUE, brightness = 1.1) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "black") +   theme_void()

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 1.4, contrast = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, aes(fill = area), color = "white") +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma")

title: Image Transformations
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,                grayscale = TRUE,                contrast = 1.3,               brightness = 0.9) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = "lightgray", color = "black") +   theme_bw()

title: Image Transformations

title: Authors and Citation
text: Name Y (2026). ggbasemap: Add Basemap Tiles ggplot2. R package version 0.1.0, https://cedricbouffard.github.io/ggbasemap.
code: @Manual{,   title = {ggbasemap: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2},   author = {Your Name},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://cedricbouffard.github.io/ggbasemap}, }

title: Nouvelles options de transformation d’image
code: # Augmenter le contraste (>1) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, contrast = 1.5) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")  # Diminuer le contraste (<1)   ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, contrast = 0.7) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")

title: Nouvelles options de transformation d’image
code: # Gamma < 1 = tons moyens plus clairs ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, gamma = 0.8) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")  # Gamma > 1 = tons moyens plus foncés ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, gamma = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")

title: Nouvelles options de transformation d’image
code: # Saturation 0 = noir et blanc ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 0) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")  # Saturation 0.5 = désaturé à 50% ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 0.5) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")  # Saturation 2 = très saturé ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")

title: Nouvelles options de transformation d’image
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,                grayscale = FALSE,               saturation = 0.7,    # Légèrement désaturé               brightness = 1.1,    # Un peu plus lumineux               contrast = 1.3,      # Plus de contraste               gamma = 0.9) +       # Tons moyens éclaircis   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "white")

title: Nouvelles options de transformation d’image
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               saturation = 0.3,    # Très désaturé               brightness = 0.9,    # Légèrement plus sombre               contrast = 1.2,      # Plus de contraste               gamma = 1.1) +       # Tons moyens assombris   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "brown")

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
text: Add XYZ tile basemaps ggplot2 automatic zoom calculation, image transformations, map rotation.

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
text: ggbasemap makes effortless add basemap tiles services like OpenStreetMap, Google, ESRI, CartoDB ggplot2 visualizations. automatically detects data extents, calculates optimal zoom levels, provides powerful image styling options.

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
text: 🗺️ Automatic Everything: Detects bounding boxes calculates optimal zoom 🎨 Image Styling: Grayscale, saturation, brightness, contrast, gamma adjustment 🔄 Map Rotation: Rotate maps angle Oblique Mercator projection 🚀 High Performance: Native Web Mercator (EPSG:3857) sharpest display 🔧 Flexible: Works sf objects, data frames, manual bounding boxes 📱 Multiple Sources: OpenStreetMap, Google, ESRI, CartoDB, custom XYZ tiles

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: # Install from CRAN (when available) install.packages("ggbasemap")  # Or the development version from GitHub # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("yourusername/ggbasemap")

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: library(ggbasemap) library(ggplot2) library(sf)  # Read spatial data nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package = "sf"))  # Create map with basemap ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red", linewidth = 1)

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: df <- data.frame(   lon = c(-74.0060, -73.9352),   lat = c(40.7128, 40.7306),   city = c("NYC", "Manhattan") )  ggplot() +   add_basemap(df, "lon", "lat") +   geom_point(data = df, aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "red", size = 3)

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: # Grayscale ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, grayscale = TRUE) +   geom_sf(data = nc)  # Styled - Vintage look ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,                saturation = 0.3,               brightness = 0.9,               contrast = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc)  # High contrast satellite ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               url = "https://mt1.google.com/vt/lyrs=y&x={x}&y={y}&z={z}",               contrast = 1.4,               gamma = 0.9) +   geom_sf(data = nc)

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: # Rotate map 45 degrees rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)  ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +   geom_sf(data = nc) +   rot$coord

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
text: OpenStreetMap (default): https://tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png Google Satellite: https://mt1.google.com/vt/lyrs=y&x={x}&y={y}&z={z} ESRI World Imagery: https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{z}/{y}/{x} CartoDB Positron: https://.basemaps.cartocdn.com/light_all/{z}/{x}/{y}.png CartoDB Dark Matter: https://.basemaps.cartocdn.com/dark_all/{z}/{x}/{y}.png OpenTopoMap: https://.tile.opentopomap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
text: add_basemap(): Add basemap tiles ggplot2 coord_rotate(): Create rotated coordinate systems

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: # Clean, minimal ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, grayscale = TRUE, contrast = 1.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "black") +   theme_void()  # Data-focused ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc, saturation = 0.2) +   geom_sf(data = nc, aes(fill = value)) +   scale_fill_viridis_c() +   theme_minimal()  # High-quality satellite ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc,               url = "ESRI_URL",               zoom = 14,               contrast = 1.3,               gamma = 0.9) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "yellow")

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(data,                grayscale = TRUE,  # Print-friendly               contrast = 1.2) +  # Better ink coverage   geom_sf(data = data) +   theme_void()

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(data,               saturation = 1.1,  # More vibrant               brightness = 1.05) + # Brighter for screens   geom_sf(data = data)

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2
code: ggplot() +   add_basemap(big_country,                zoom = 6,        # Lower zoom               padding = 0.05) + # Less padding   geom_sf(data = big_country)

what: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2 — add_basemap
title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2 — add_basemap
text: Adds basemap tiles XYZ tile server ggplot2 plot. Uses ggspatial proper coordinate management tile stitching. Automatically calculates appropriate zoom level.

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2 — add_basemap
code: add_basemap(   data = NULL,   x = NULL,   y = NULL,   bbox = NULL,   crs = 4326,   url = "https://tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png",   zoom = NULL,   alpha = 1,   interpolate = TRUE,   padding = 0.1,   grayscale = FALSE,   saturation = 1,   brightness = 1,   contrast = 1,   gamma = 1,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2 — add_basemap
text: data data frame sf object containing spatial data (optional bbox provided) x name longitude column. Can unquoted (bare name) quoted (character string). Optional data sf object bbox provided. y name latitude column. Can unquoted (bare name) quoted (character string). Optional data sf object bbox provided. bbox Bounding box c(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax) sf bbox object. Optional data provided. crs Coordinate reference system (default: 4326 WGS84). Can EPSG code crs object. url XYZ tile URL template x, y, z placeholders zoom Zoom level (optional, auto-calculated provided) alpha Transparency basemap (0-1) interpolate Whether interpolate raster image padding Fraction range add padding around data (default 0.1) grayscale Convert image grayscale (default FALSE) saturation Saturation multiplier (1 = change, 0 = grayscale, >1 = saturated, default 1) brightness Brightness multiplier (1 = change, <1 = darker, >1 = brighter, default 1) contrast Contrast multiplier (1 = change, <1 = less contrast, >1 = contrast, default 1) gamma Gamma correction value (1 = change, <1 = brighter midtones, >1 = darker midtones, default 1) verbose Print debugging information (default FALSE)

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2 — add_basemap
text: ggplot2 layer (list layers) can added plot

title: Add Basemap Tiles to ggplot2 — add_basemap
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(ggplot2)  # Method 1: Using a data frame with bare column names df <- data.frame(lon = c(-74.0, -73.9), lat = c(40.7, 40.8)) ggplot() +   add_basemap(df, lon, lat) +   geom_point(data = df, aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "red", size = 3)  # Method 1b: Using a data frame with quoted column names ggplot() +   add_basemap(df, "lon", "lat") +   geom_point(data = df, aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "red", size = 3)  # Method 2: Using an sf object library(sf) nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package="sf")) ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc) +   geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red")  # Method 3: Using ggspatial layer_spatial ggplot() +   add_basemap(nc) +   layer_spatial(nc, fill = NA, color = "red")  # Method 4: Using bbox directly bbox <- c(xmin = -74.1, ymin = 40.6, xmax = -73.8, ymax = 40.9) ggplot() +   add_basemap(bbox = bbox) +   geom_point(data = df, aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "red", size = 3) } # }
